text:
CRDO was far too oversold on the initial photonics panic and we have remained bullish through it all, adding our position back into the decline.

Today Credo announced the acquisition of DustPhotonics, an Israeli-based pioneer in Silicon Photonics. This $750 million+ land grab is designed to transform Credo from a DSP/SerDes component seller into an Optical Powerhouse capable of competing directly with the Broadcom-Marvell duopoly in the 1.6T and 3.2T eras. By acquiring DustPhotonics, they are bringing Photonic Integrated Circuit (PIC) design in-house. DustPhotonics PICs integrate lasers, modulators, and detectors onto a single silicon chip & their roadmap extends directly to 3.2T Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) and Near-Port Optics (NPO). This is a vertical integration move, as previously, Credo had to source SiPho PICs from third parties, creating a margin and supply chain dependency.

Credo is up an eye watering 24% today including the move after the close.

text:
$CRDO
Credo: to acquire DustPhotonics in $750M optical connectivity deal:
- Deal valued at $750M plus stock consideration 
- Includes 0.92M shares and up to 3.21M earnout shares - Expands capabilities in silicon photonics and high-speed connectivity 
- DustPhotonics supports 400G, 800G, 1.6T and future 3.2T solutions 
- Combined optical segment projected to exceed $500M revenue by FY2027 
- Enhances exposure to AI data center networking demand 
- Target operates fabless with ~70 employees

text:
Samsung Foundry just entered the silicon photonics market.
300mm platform. PDK ready. 2029 CPO turnkey roadmap.
Device performance? Competitive.
MRM 74GHz. MZM VpiL 1.4V·cm.
But here is the problem.
Zero customers. Zero production history.
TSMC COUPE is already in 2026 production qualification.
NVIDIA Spectrum-X confirmed.
The gap: roughly 3 years.
And here is the part most investors are missing.
The foundry generating the most SiPh revenue today is not TSMC.
Q4 run rate: $380M/year.
5x capacity expansion underway. 70% pre-booked with customer prepayments.
Who it is, why they are hard to beat, and whether Samsung's vertical integration card actually works.
Full analysis inside.
$TSM $GFS $TSEM $NVDA $AVGO
Samsung's Silicon Photonics Bet: Too Late Against TSMC, GFS and Tower?

text:
POET Technologies is squarely in the FUTURE column. Their Optical Interposer platform integrates photonics + electronics on silicon (CMOS-style), enabling exactly what the diagram shows: light built into GPU packages, lower power, more chiplets, no external lasers wasting energy, and faster AI data center interconnects.

They're past pure R&D—$5M+ production orders in, 30k+ optical engines shipping 2026, 800G engines Q3, 1.6T prototypes late 2026. Revenue ramp starts now, but full hyperscale scale is 2027+. Solid play on the shift.

This company was always overlooked due to photonics fear. Photonics could never eat their business.  

Too central a position in the infrastructure space and too much cash flow with increasing margins. 

Now with photonics arm integrated they already have the customers and distribution, if customers demand photonics they have the trust and scale to deliver. 

The floor here is incredibly high and the ceiling just got raised. One of the safest bets in the market.

text:
Wonder if the chance that scaling up with Photonics will be required, and not just for the scale out is priced in.

Maine won’t be the only state to block data center builds due to energy consumption and thermal regulation advantages could drive use in scaling up if mass production can be efficiently achieved.

I’d also wonder how many of the “canceled” data centers got word that “hey your current schematics won’t work for where we are going” so it’s forced to go back to a rebid.

This stuff is fairly expensive to screw up so although CapEx is urgent it’s not without strategy.

text:
$IQE / $IQEPF represents a non-negotiable manufacturing reality: 

You can’t build the AI photonics supply chain without compound semiconductor epiwafers.

No epiwafers = No lasers. No lasers = No 1.6T/3.2T interconnects. No interconnects = No AI scaling. 

They’re not just a supplier; they’re the global dominant floor.

text:
✏️LESSON EXTRACTION FROM SUCCESSFUL PITCH

Soitec ( $SOI.PA) by @jasons_chips 

121% gain since the March 10 pitch.

📋 WHAT THE PITCH ARGUED:
🔹 Mobile segment was at a cyclical floor with inventory clearing.
🔹 "Smart Cut" technology created a near-monopoly in photonics substrates.
🔹 Management was deliberately low-balling future growth projections.

✅ WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED:
🔹 Q3 sequential revenue beat confirmed the mobile trough was in.
🔹 1.6T networking demand pulled photonics orders forward significantly.

🎯 THE SURPRISE:
The market re-rated the stock as a pure AI infrastructure play almost overnight. It shifted from a "boring" materials cyclical to a critical networking bottleneck provider.

MY TAKE: We expected a slow recovery as inventory cleared. Instead, NVIDIA's commentary on Co-Packaged Optics triggered a massive institutional rotation into the name. This shows how quickly a value setup turns into a momentum play when the macro tailwind arrives.
But if I am honest, this performance is likely a result of important people mentioning this company on X 😀

AXTI, TSEM, GFS: AXTI down 6.5% on its own supply-demand timing mismatch, not thesis deterioration. TSEM and GFS caught a modest SiPho/specialty foundry bid but lack the direct InP laser catalyst.

2. Photonic Integrated Circuits & Early-Stage Materials

ALMU: Carrying Monday's $4M+ government contract momentum into today. Quantum dot laser and nonlinear photonic materials funding is direct AI datacom and quantum compute positioning. Watch the AngelTech panel April 20.

POET, LWLG, BESIY: Risk-on sympathy trade. Today's catalyst is InP-specific, so EO polymer and other platform stories get lower beta. No fundamental change.

Morningstar noted that Soitec's shares rose 50% in just five trading sessions as investors priced in a bullish outlook for the firm's silicon photonics segment. Morningstar (https://t.co/djE1rX2T9F)
The broader sector is being reshaped by a clear bifurcation: capital is rotating into names with tangible substrate and test infrastructure exposure to the AI optical buildout, while pre-revenue photonic integration plays are getting punished. $POET dropped after Wolfpack Research disclosed a short position, calling the company "an obvious stock promotion" and flagging potential PFIC tax liabilities for U.S. shareholders ahead of the April 15 filing deadline. https://t.co/RRfdqNGIy4 $SIVEF fell in sympathy as the short report reinforced broader skepticism toward pre-revenue photonic chip names. Meanwhile, SPX +2.21% and NDX +1.66% confirm this is a risk-on tape where the photonics laggards are underperforming on idiosyncratic, not macro, weakness.

1. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
$SOI, $BESIY, $TSEM, $AXTI: The substrate tier is where capital is concentrating. SOI's parabolic move reflects the market treating Photonics-SOI wafers as a structural bottleneck for CPO adoption. BESIY (BE Semiconductor) rode the same wave on die-attach and advanced packaging exposure. TSEM continues to benefit from specialty foundry demand, while AXTI held green on InP substrate scarcity narrative despite thin volume.
$IQE, $GFS: IQE gave back -2.65% as the OTC liquidity drag persists despite the same end-market tailwinds. GFS slipped -1.28%, still digesting the auto/industrial overhang even as its RF-SOI partnership with Soitec positions it well longer term.
2. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
$COHR, $MRVL: Coherent and Marvell both green, tracking the 800G/1.6T transceiver cycle. COHR +1.80% is steady rather than explosive here, which suggests the easy money in pluggable transceiver names may already be priced.
$LITE, $CIEN, $AAOI: The transceiver and networking names that should be benefiting from the same AI optical build are instead lagging. LITE -2.15% and CIEN -2.68% are giving back recent gains. AAOI -4.41% continues to trade heavy, potentially reflecting profit-taking after its tariff-exemption-driven run.
$GLW, $VIAV: Passive optical infrastructure names drifting lower. Not broken, just not where the heat is today.
3. Semiconductor Equipment, Test and Infrastructure
$AEHR, $FORM, $KEYS, $TER, $ATEYY: AEHR +3.84% is the standout, continuing its recent strength on wafer-level burn-in demand tied to SiC and compound semi production ramps. ATEYY (Advantest) surged +8.65%, a strong signal that test infrastructure spending for advanced packaging and photonics ICs is accelerating. FORM flat, KEYS and TER slightly red but neither signaling anything concerning.
4. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
$LWLG, $ALMU, $POET, $SIVEF: LWLG bucked the early-stage selloff with a +7.20% move, potentially on renewed interest in EO polymer modulators as CPO architectures demand novel modulator solutions beyond conventional LiNbO3. ALMU -7.36% gave back gains on thin float dynamics. POET -8.01% is the direct casualty of the Wolfpack short report, which weaponized the PFIC tax angle right before the April 15 deadline. text:
我理解的SIVE
聊完AEHR 和 AAOI 还有很大的涨幅空间
来聊聊目前上涨空间最大的一个公司SIVE
今天SIVE就+30%

SIVE

这个公司basically就是做让AI芯片传输数据的激光器
AI芯片是需要光用silicon photonics技术来传输数据的

为什么要用光来传输数据呢？
当电信号的频率极高的时候 电子不再在整根铜线内部跑 而是在铜线的表面跑 会让电阻指数级别飙升
数据中心的两张显卡交换数据的时候速度会达到1.6T
这么快的速度电信号跑不到1-2米就会衰减成没有办法识别的代码
而且为了对抗铜线的衰减，只能加大电压去推信号，产生的热量很高不仅费电还危险
最重要最重要的一点是光没有质量+没有电荷
在玻璃里传输的时候没有电阻
总结一句话来说就是
铜线的带宽是有极限的 而光的带宽是无限的
这也是为什么我前几年最大持仓的CRDO有压力的原因

在实验室做一个好激光器不难 但是难在100毫米的inp晶圆上 想量产laser arrays(我都不知道这个中文叫什么 like 激光器矩阵？somebody teach me）并且质量很高 是非常难的 所以大公司都找sive定

现在他们确定的订单有50M预定订单有450M
2028 2029年
我预估300M营收 x 20ps 5-6B的市值 这还是很保守的
今天市值才660M
660M x 10-15？

聊聊为什么还有很高空间 排除公司本身
1. 这个公司并没有在美国nasdaq上市
而是在瑞典nasdaq上市 来这边上市估计股价更高
想在fidelity上买还要加50🔪
2. 市值太小 like 660M的市值我都没办法布置sizeable 仓位 更别说其他manage billions的fund manager
3. 还有个x因素 x上一个很火的大神 @aleabitoreddit  粉丝量很大 分析ai公司非常厉害
我也在关注 好像他成sive 1%+的大股东了

我的预测 sive会被大公司收购 可能瞬间10x
我预估Avgo或者Marvell吧 这两家最有可能收购他 来补自己的短板

我的public accounts 持仓更新完整在图2:
Sive没有放进去因为大部分朋友自己的broker买不到
BE +53%
Aehr+59%
aaoi+74%
mu+30%
lwlg+45%
ichr+20%
uct+ 24%
等等

Join me
我所有持仓都将公开

$sive

text:
GAN is the way forward … lets discuss some alpha in the Photonics Supercycle: Signals in AIXA earnings call

$AIXA had earnings call and popped up another +11%, bringing YTD upside to +130% and 1 yr upside to +288%.

You guys know that am already invested in Aixtron ($AIXA) and have written about this German champion in the past, framing it as a quintessential "hidden monopoly" within the semiconductor equipment space. I also think that this is early innings of a massive Photonics Supercycle. As AI clusters migrate from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T optical modules, the bottleneck for AI scaling has shifted from the H100/B200 compute engine to the interconnect fabric itself. You cannot run a frontier model if the data can’t move between the GPU and the memory stack fast enough. Aixtron sits at the very mouth of this funnel.

I would not regurgitate the numbers here, rather analyse and unpack the things I consider important with impact on overall AI and semisector. 

The Great Divergence: GaN vs. SiC

One of the most tactical takeaways from the call was the stark contrast between Gallium Nitride (GaN) and Silicon Carbide (SiC).

🐊 SiC Overcapacity: Management was candid about Silicon Carbide being in a state of "substantial market overcapacity," with SiC expected to be only 10% of their revenue this year. This is a warning shot for pure-play SiC substrate and device makers who are still digesting inventory.

🐊 The GaN Opportunity: Conversely, GaN is entering a golden era. Management expects AI data centers to become the largest single application for GaN power semiconductors. Because GaN is significantly more efficient than traditional silicon at high frequencies, it is becoming the standard for the power supply units (PSUs) that feed power-hungry AI servers.

For those looking to play the GaN trend beyond the equipment layer, keep an eye on these players:

• Navitas Semiconductor ($NVTS) – A US-based pure-play leader in GaN power ICs.

• Infineon Technologies ($IFX.DE / $IFNNY) – A European giant with a massive, growing footprint in GaN following their acquisition of GaN Systems.

• STMicroelectronics ($STM) – Another European heavyweight with strong GaN-on-Silicon capabilities.

• BluGlass ($BLG.AX) – An Australian-listed speculative play involved in specialized

🐊 Datacom Doubling: Management is forecasting that demand for datacom lasers will more than double YoY in 2026. This is a massive green flag for the laser makers themselves, such as Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR), who are racing to expand Indium Phosphide (InP) capacity. (am invested in both)

🐊The "Tool of Record" for AI Interconnects

The standout signal from the call was the performance of the G10-AsP system. Management confirmed it has become the "tool of record" for the next generation of photonic components

🐊 Order Intake Explosion: Order intake hit €171 million, a ~30% YoY increase that blew past analyst expectations. Crucially, 65% of these orders came from the optoelectronics segment

🐊Implications for the Memory (HBM) Ecosystem

While we often view memory through the lens of SK Hynix or Micron $MU, the Aixtron update provides a critical "read-through" for how these chips will be connected in the future.

🐊 Structural Bottlenecks: For those holding SK Hynix, $DRAM or Micron ($MU), the shift toward optical interconnects is the solution to the "power wall" that threatens to limit HBM performance. Aixtron’s order backlog suggests the equipment for this transition is being installed now.

🐊 Near-Stack Integration: The acceleration of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) means that optical engines will eventually sit directly on the package alongside High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks.

Look the "Photonics Supercycle" isn't a theory anymore it’s appearing in the capex cycles of the world's largest chipmakers.

And remember, none of this is financial advice. The crocodile can make errors. So do your own research.

Aixtron’s order backlog suggests the equipment for this transition is being installed now. 🐊 Near-Stack Integration: The acceleration of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) means that optical engines will eventually sit directly on the package alongside High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks. Look the "Photonics Supercycle" isn't a theory anymore it’s appearing in the capex cycles of the world's largest chipmakers. And remember, none of this is financial advice. The crocodile can make errors. $AAOI looks a bit extended but no denying the strength both in the photonics basket as well as this name specifically. 200 should come in short order if this holds up.

Two ways to play it: retest of the 10d SMA for the lower-risk entry, or a breakout through 160.

Watch LITE, GLW and the rest of the photonics basket for confirmation, always best to see names within a group/theme move together.

text:
Frontrunning 1.6T/CPO within the broader photonics supercycle is the most compelling investment to me. 

I have high conviction in that statement.

Which is why I'm long the entire supply chain (+1 extra bottlenecK)

1. $SIVE - Their laser revenue scales aggressively with $JBL, $MRVL, Ayar, O-Net.

And I do think CPO/1.6T will blow away any conservative analyst projections from how hard $NVDA, $GOOGL, and others have been pushing photonics architectures.

Downside risk is multi-sourcing, but there's a reason Jabil chose Sivers. When you compare $MTSI, $LITE, $COHR, Furukawa, and others.

There's genuinely not many laser suppliers in the entire world... they're all $10B+, then you have this mini CHIPS act chokepoint trading at <$1B MC.

2. Shunsin (6451) - I don't see how it's possible Foxconn's optical foundry for testing, packaging, and assembly is valued at $1.5B MC less than $LWLG. 

When they look extremely derisked piggybacking off of Foxconn's photonics volume.

$TSM's optical arm VisEra example is ~$5B, but they scale H2 2028 from Gen-3. Foxconn looks to be ramping up just next year.

They're just scaling low fwd p/e multiples off of $NVDA CPO supply chain demand in Taiwan and all public indicators point to capacity expansion + extreme demand. 
 
3. Win Semi - They're the foundry for Sivers to scale up DFB laser production. As well as $AVGO, SpaceX supply chains and others.

When I do supply chain mapping and Win Semi pops up in every single frontier supply chain I see. 

There's probably something markets are not pricing in.

4. $MRVL - I find this genuinely compelling as a mini-Broadcomm. 

Their potential design with with $GOOGL today, helps the case past 2028.

But the catalyst I was looking at was $MSFT Maia ramp, which happens H2 2026, and likely keep scaling up exponentially into 2027, 2028, 2029. 

Celestial acquisition was probably the smartest thing in the world for them. Maybe on next drop or CSP? 

5. $HPS.A - Transformers/Switchgears are commodities  + boring parts of the DC supply chain.

However, when the bottleneck is 2-5 years, and you have backlog increasing 100%+... causing extreme shortages.

It's only up 20%+ since my thesis post, but I do see this being de-risked given massive backlog visibility (even though it's inferred, they don't give exact #). 

I do think markets are missing something, especially with potential gross margin expansion from price hikes if they pull it off.... 

Again backlog + demand just de-risks this company,  and it seems like a high growth compounder post facility expansion last year. 

There's many others like $NBIS, $JBL, $RPI, $TSEM, $LITE, $ARM, $SOI, $AXTI,  $IQE, $ALRIB, Fittech, PCL, and others that I'm very fond of, but just mentioning 5 off the top of my head from today's prices... if I'm creating a new portfolio. 

Of course, it's good to barbell with other uncorrelated companies to AI supply chains, but these are just 5 I liked.

{"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "THE OPTICAL PHOTONICS BOTTLENECK\n\nAs AI clusters scale past copper’s physical limits, the bottleneck shifts to optical & these are the companies building that layer across the stack: \n\n1. $AAOI building the transceiver layer of the AI network through vertically integrated U.S.-based InP laser manufacturing. It has already secured over $200M in its first volume 1.6T order from one hyperscale customer followed by another $124M in 800G orders from a second.\n\n2. $AEHR building the reliability layer for the optical & AI hardware stack through burn-in & test systems. It just received a record $41M follow-on order from its lead hyperscale customer reinforcing the idea that Sonoma is becoming a key production burn-in platform for high-power AI ASICs.\n\n3. $CRDO building the connectivity layer that helps AI clusters move data faster through active electrical cables, retimers & high-speed interconnect silicon. The DustPhotonics acquisition also extends that platform into silicon photonics before copper becomes a real constraint.\n\n4. $LITE building the laser layer of the AI optical stack through EMLs, optical components & optical switching exposure. The setup is backed by a $2B $NVDA strategic investment & optical circuit switch backlog above $400M with orders reportedly extending through 2028.\n\n5. $VIAV building the testing & validation layer of the optical stack through network instrumentation & photonics measurement tools. It is the picks-and-shovels layer of the transition because every high-speed optical buildout still needs to be tested regardless of which transceiver vendor wins.\n\n6. $COHR building one of the core photonics bottlenecks through indium phosphide lasers, optical engines & communications components tied to next-gen AI networking. It also has a $2B $NVDA strategic investment behind it & is doubling InP device capacity into the 1.6T ramp.\n\n7. $MRVL building the DSP & optical infrastructure layer through electro-optics, PAM DSPs, interconnect silicon & custom networking chips. The Celestial AI deal & NVLink Fusion exposure both strengthen its position as photonics becomes more central to AI cluster design."}}}, "rest_id": "2045885512922419251", "source": "<a href=\"https://mobile.twitter.com\" rel=\"nofollow\">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "146342", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2046003571603423359", "source": "<a href=\"https://mobile.twitter.com\" rel=\"nofollow\">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "8712", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
This map shows why Sivers - ( $SIVE ) is quietly becoming one of the most important names in AI photonics.

Sivers sits in the center of the CPO ecosystem supplying the critical multi-wavelength laser arrays to:

•  Jabil (1.6T LRO pluggables)
•  Ayar Labs (optical I/O)
•  O-Net + Enablence (external light source modules)
•  POET (hybrid integration, now tied to Marvell + Google TPU discussions)

That’s direct or indirect exposure to $META , $AMZN , $MSFT , $GOOGL , and $MRVL — basically the entire hyperscaler map.

Still only ~$720M market cap

The Nasdaq listing is coming. The commercial ramps are coming.

Here's why I think this $6B space pioneer is an asymmetrical bet. I love $FLY because its business spans the full orbital stack: roughly 20% launch, 40% spacecraft, and 40% defense software. On the spacecraft side, $FLY’s Blue Ghost lander makes it the only company in history to land successfully on the moon. A 10 year old space startup achieved the same feat only 5 countries had before. It now has $487M in contracted NASA missions and CEO Jason Kim has said Firefly is in discussions with NASA about block buy contracts for 5 to 10 landers, which would represent between $500 million and $1.4 billion in a single order. That’s asymmetric growth against a company doing $160 million in trailing revenue. $FLY also has a spacecraft called Elytra which is this swiss army knife of sorts. It’s part tugboat, part sentinel. It launches payloads, deploys them, then stays on station for years as a comms relay, surveillance platform, or deployer. What I love about Elytra is that it’s about to monopolize lunar imaging using Nvidia Jetson compute which can create high-margin revenue to map mining and landing sites as we work to colonize the moon. Now the real leverage with $FLY is that it also builds its own rockets. It has a small lift rocket called Alpha that’s done seven launches (it’s worth noting only 3 were successful, but $RKLB’s first Electron flights were also rocky) and has a medium lift rocket Eclipse launching in early 2027 co-developed with Northrup Grumman. This timeline isn’t too far off $RKLB’s delayed Neutron vehicle now Q4 2026. The key point here is that Firefly’s spacecraft will one day own the highway on which they travel. As the launch business progresses, it’s de-risked by hundreds of millions worth of lunar landing contracts, helping boost margins on the primary spacecraft division. Vertical integration at its finest. Don’t get me wrong, $FLY is significantly behind its comparable $RKLB in the launch market. It’s a Ford to a Ferrari. But here's what many retail investors interested in space need to understand. Space companies like $RKLB and SpaceX are not being priced on their launch businesses as much as they are their higher margin divisions. $RKLB’s premium multiple is justified by their space systems division and specifically the components business (60-70% gross margins) or with SpaceX, Starlink. That’s where $FLY’s defense software becomes so important. I think it’s the primary vector on which the market is mispricing Firefly. Firefly bought a missile defense AI software company called SciTec in November that brought $164M in trailing revenue, about equal to $FLY’s entire 2025 haul and about 40% of 2026 guidance in an accretive transaction that was mostly stock at $50/share. This business is EBITDA and cash flow positive, a rarity in any space economy unit. It has a $372M FORGE contract with the Space Force and has major upside with the $185B Golden Dome initiative. If 40% of $FLY’s revenue is coming from an extremely fast growing Palantir-esque defense software company with margins that are presumably much higher than anything Rocket Lab is doing in space systems… Then why should $FLY trade at such a steep discount to $RKLB? Just 12.5x forward EV/Revenue compared to 43x for $RKLB. I think as the market better grasps the SciTec opportunity, we could see $FLY re-rate to at least half of $RKLB’s multiple, presenting major upside over the current share price. Of course there are massive risks with this idea. $FLY isn’t expected to deliver positive EPS until 2028, while comps like $RKLB and $LUNR are expected to do so in 2027. Over $800M in cash is a decent war chest but dilution will happen as the company has maxed out its credit facility, so investors should be prepared for ATMs. A shitty launch record with Alpha doesn’t instill major confidence for the launch business or the future synergies of that business on the rest of the pie, but it's still early and RKLB electron had early stage issues too. Launch commoditization is a risk here but it's mitigated by the massive pipeline of NASA lunar lander contracts if Eclipse can service some of those payloads. Private equity company AE industrial partners is also a big governance risk, with 3 board seats and majority voting control. They forced a bad acquisition on $RDW, which diluted shareholders. Finally, there’s just massive execution risks in any space business that can lead to significant delays combined with lumpy government contracting revenue that can lead to earnings misses. Overall, I think $FLY is the most complete small cap space package that the market is still sleeping on. It owns the moon, the rockets, and the software. And if the SpaceX IPO is supposed to re-rate the entire space sector, isn’t $FLY the biggest direct beneficiary since the mission is now the Moon and not Mars? It’s the space stock most primed to directly assist SpaceX with its primary IPO objective. Check out my full Substack deep dive below to really wrap your head around $FLY, which goes into way more detail. Investors can sleep so soundly holding a company like that lmao.

text:
Credo (CRDO) just vaulted into AI optics with DustPhotonics, echoing Marvell’s silicon‑photonics playbook.

Credo (CRDO) advances next as DustPhotonics adds ready optics to its active cable and ZeroFlap lineup. This holds because hyperscalers now prioritize in‑rack to between‑rack optical links for faster AI cluster buildouts. Today’s strong after‑hours pop reflects the headline, while multi‑quarter design wins and module ramps remain only partly shown in price action this week. DSPs and optical modules shift from nice‑to‑have to must‑have as rack bandwidth climbs. Peer precedence from Marvell’s Celestial AI deal supports a rerate window. Memory‑to‑compute bandwidth needs keep optics at the center for AI rollouts.

text:
$CRDO is acquiring DustPhotonics in a $750M silicon photonics deal that also includes stock. 

The deal expands Credo deeper into optical connectivity across 400G to 3.2T with the combined optical business expected to exceed $500M in FY2027.

BOOM 💥

$AAOI and $SNDK:

Are the perfect examples why you don’t follow hedge funds named after Tangerine peels…

Like if you’re going short, do it with companies with terrible fundamentals.

Not two of the largest beneficiaries of the photonics and memory supercycles?

I struggle to understand the reasoning behind their trades.

text:
💡Credo $CRDO 、イスラエルSiPho企業DustPhotonicsを7.5億ドルで買収 | Silicon Photonicsの垂直統合へ踏み出す

Credo Technology $CRDO が、Silicon Photonics（SiPho）Photonic Integrated Circuit（PIC）の開発企業DustPhotonicsの買収で最終合意に達した。2026年4月13日付の発表によれば、対価は現金7.5億ドルと約92万株のCredo普通株で構成され、さらに一定の財務マイルストーン達成に応じて最大約321万株の追加対価が支払われる可能性がある。クロージングは2026年第2四半期（暦年）を見込み、FY2027（Credoの会計年度）における非GAAP EPSへのアクレティブ効果を期待している。

この買収の核心は、Credoの接続ポートフォリオにおける「光」領域の垂直統合にある。同社はすでにSerDes IP、DSP、ZeroFlap（ZF）Optical Transceiver、Active Electrical Cable（AEC）といった電気・光接続の両面で製品群を構築してきたが、光トランシーバの中核部品であるSiPho PICは外部調達に依存していた。DustPhotonicsの技術を内製化することで、400G/800G/1.6T対応のPICを自社スタック内に取り込み、3.2Tまでのロードマップ拡張も視野に入る。Credoはこの統合により、外部サプライ依存の軽減、開発サイクルの短縮、量産時のコスト構造改善を見込んでいる。

DustPhotonicsはイスラエル・モディイン（Modi'in）に本社を構えるファブレス半導体企業で、2017年の創業以来、光の機能をシリコンチップ上に集積するSiPho PIC技術に注力してきた。会長のAvigdor WillenzはHabana LabsをIntelへ、Annapurna LabsをAWSへ売却した実績で知られるイスラエル半導体業界の重鎮であり、DustPhotonicsにも創業初期から投資してきた。同社は累計1億ドル超を調達しており、投資家にはGreenfield Partners、Intel Capital、Sienna Venture Capital、Exor Ventures、そしてAtreides Managementが名を連ねる。従業員数は約70名。取締役にはWRVI Capital創設者でIntel元取締役のLip-Bu Tanも参画していた。

なお、買収発表の前日にはイスラエルのCalcalistが、DustPhotonicsが数億ドル規模の売却交渉を進めており、Intel、NVIDIA $NVDA 、Amazonが候補に挙がっていると報じていた。結果的にCredoが獲得した形だが、主要テック企業が争奪戦を繰り広げていたことは、SiPho PIC技術への業界全体の関心の高さを物語っている。

市場の反応は即座だった。発表を受けて $CRDO は約12%上昇し、134ドル台に到達している。Credoの直近四半期（FQ2 2026）の売上高は約4.07億ドル、EPSは1.07ドル（予想0.91ドルに対し17.4%のサプライズ）と好調であり、年間売上成長率は前年比+201.5%を記録していた。時価総額は約199億ドル。Credoは今回の買収を含む光事業の拡大により、ZF Optical Transceiver、Optical DSP、SiPho製品を合わせた光関連売上がFY2027に5億ドルを超えると見込んでいる。

SiPho PIC市場全体については、LightCountingとCredoの推計で2030年までに60億ドル規模に成長すると予測されている。AIクラスタの規模拡大に伴い、ポート速度が800Gを超えて1.6T、3.2Tへと移行する過程で、SiPho PICは従来のプラガブルトランシーバに比べて低消費電力・低コスト・高信頼性を実現する構造的優位性を持つ。Near Port Optics（NPO）やCo-Packaged Optics（CPO）といった次世代実装形態においても、SiPho PICは基盤技術としての位置づけを強めている。

📍今後の展望
Credoのこの一手は、AI接続インフラの競争地図を塗り替える可能性を持つ。SerDes IPからDSP、SiPho PIC、光トランシーバ、AECまでを一社で提供できるフルスタック接続プラットフォームは、Broadcom $AVGO やMarvell $MRVL といった競合にはない独自のポジショニングとなる。ハイパースケーラーにとって、AIクラスタの信頼性と電力効率は最優先課題であり、Credoの垂直統合はまさにその2点に直接応える戦略である。

一方、リスクも存在する。7.5億ドルの現金支出はCredoの手元現金12.2億ドル（直近四半期末）の約6割に相当し、バランスシートへの影響は小さくない。また、70名規模のイスラエル拠点の技術チームの統合と、ファブレスモデルでの量産拡大という実行面での課題も残る。FY2027の光事業5億ドル超という強気な見通しが実現するかどうかは、2026年6月3日に予定される次回決算が最初の検証ポイントとなるだろう。

text:
$CRDO is acquiring DustPhotonics in a $750M silicon photonics deal 

The deal structure is more complex than the headline $750M suggests:

•$750M in cash upfront
•~0.92 million CRDO shares at closing
•Up to ~3.3 million additional CRDO shares as earnout tied to specific financial milestones
•Expected close: Q2 calendar 2026, pending regulatory approval

•EPS impact: Accretive to non-GAAP EPS in fiscal year 2027
•Combined optical revenue target: Over $500M in fiscal 2027

Before this deal, Credo was a DSP (Digital Signal Processor) company that designed the brains of optical interconnects but had to source the photonics from third-party suppliers. DustPhotonics fills the exact missing layer. Post-acquisition, Credo controls the entire vertical stack

This vertical integration is critical because Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) — the next frontier of AI chip design where optics are packaged directly onto the GPU/switch die requires exactly this kind of end-to-end control. Credo now has it

Most switch ports are already at 800 Gbps and racing toward 1.6T and 3.2T.
DustPhotonics already has a 3.2T roadmap. Credo just bought years of R&D and the industry’s only 1.6T merchant silicon photonics engine.

The risk: DustPhotonics has never disclosed revenue.
$500M combined optical revenue target for FY2027 = aggressive.
The earnout structure tells you Credo knows this ,they’re hedging.

•Combined optical revenue target: Over $500M in fiscal 2027 Before this deal, Credo was a DSP (Digital Signal Processor) company that designed the brains of optical interconnects but had to source the photonics from third-party suppliers. DustPhotonics fills the exact missing layer. Post-acquisition, Credo controls the entire vertical stack This vertical integration is critical because Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) — the next frontier of AI chip design where optics are packaged directly onto the GPU/switch die requires exactly this kind of end-to-end control. Credo now has it Most switch ports are already at 800 Gbps and racing toward 1.6T and 3.2T. DustPhotonics already has a 3.2T roadmap. Credo just bought years of R&D and the industry’s only 1.6T merchant silicon photonics engine. The risk: DustPhotonics has never disclosed revenue. $500M combined optical revenue target for FY2027 = aggressive. The earnout structure tells you Credo knows this ,they’re hedging."}}}, "rest_id": "2043806302389125539", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "76", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

FinX has been so consumed with the likes of 

$SOFI $HOOD $HIMS $ZETA $NBIS $IREN and the such 

That it completely missed out on the photonics plays bottlenecking AI in $LITE $COHR $AAOI $MRVL

Nokia’s bull thesis (as of April 2026): $NOK

The $2.3B Infinera acquisition has created a vertically integrated optical/photonics powerhouse addressing AI data center bottlenecks with high-speed coherent pluggables (1.6T+), lower power, and scalable DCI fabrics. 

OFC 2026 demos and strong hyperscaler orders (+17% optical growth) signal acceleration in the 11–18% CAGR interconnect market.

Combined with NVIDIA’s $1B investment and AI-RAN trials (T-Mobile, SoftBank), Nokia builds end-to-end AI-native networks from wireless edge (RAN leadership + 6G foundations) to optical core—few peers match this stack.

2026 guidance provides a floor; optical/IP ramps (10–12%+ potential) plus margin expansion could drive 10–15%+ EPS growth and rerating to growth multiples.

I’m seeing some pushback on an equipment level, and I want to explain a little bit on that. @vlmkapital wrote a good post on how he views the investment case from the equipement level (link in comments) and I want to expand on things there are missing. Heads up.. this one is longer than usual. Most of the pushback I’m seeing X on sectors is superficial, and the argument I’m making here has multiple layers that all depend on each other. So if you want the short version, you won’t find it here💁‍♂️ @GyujinAAIG is working on the full scope for AAIG, and will publish the research soon but I will lay out the strategy part from my side already a bit. The lens that is being used when tracking companies like LPKF and the timelines is a technology adoption kind of lens. Will give @FinnStockinger also a tag in this part. But they’re missing the capital allocation and incentive lens, which is freaking crucial for this thesis. I’m going to explain a little bit why, and later I’m also going to use Aehr Test Systems as some kind of example of what happened there. So what’s the situation on the ground now? Semco expects the glass substrate era to enter into full swing around 2027-ish. They are currently developing samples with customers, and Absolics is also targeting mass production in 2027 and moving ahead with preparation. They got SKC approval for a capital increase of around $690 million, allocating roughly $410 million into Absolics for product development. So there are real commitments there, no doubt. Also, I’m not saying to totally ignore the glass thesis. We are watching it closely as well. I’m just pointing out some crucial things. If you look carefully at what is actually being built, Absolics is building a facility of around 12,000 square meters and is in some pre-qualification discussions with AMD and AWS. That’s not HVM. That is a very expensive demonstration phase, which is totally normal. It happens a lot. Companies build pilot lines, run sample production, and do customer qualification runs. It’s an investment to stay in the technology race, but it’s not revenue-generating HVM deployment. Keep that in mind. The reality is that it’s a qualification facility running at a small fraction of the scale needed to move the industry and the economics. I will elaborate further on that a bit later. Semco and Absolics are substrate manufacturers, and they’re building capacity for a market whose architectural gateway has not yet opened. It actually has been delayed. And this is where people keep getting confused — substrate manufacturer ramp-up plans are not the same thing as equipment-supplier inflection. SEMCO and Absolics don’t decide whether glass enters the HVM stack at scale. They’re positioning themselves for it. Two very different things."}}}, "rest_id": "2045604137866551549", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "18989", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045611455228047700", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3525", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "262628", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045589392387920373", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "6582", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Fiber laser with anti-collision system made in Poland. Kimla builds their fiber lasers using US/German supplier IPG Photonics for the source. Most of the machine is from components made in Poland. It's actually clean, it doesn't fund the Chinese.
embedded_text:
Building a fast fiber laser is easy
protecting it from collisions at  speed is the actual problem

Kimla Anti collision system reacts 20X faster than the competition 

linear motors + height sensors that doesnt lag speed 
without safety is just expensive downtime simply waiting https://t.co/e6MNiVB5wO

text:
As of April 13, 2026
The AI Photonics & Optical Infrastructure sector is showing strong bullish momentum, led by a massive surge in $ALMU. Aeluma secured over $4 million in non-dilutive U.S. government contracts to scale its heterogeneous integration platform for quantum dot lasers and AlGaAs nonlinear photonics, partnering with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo Chemical Advanced Technology on wafer production. (https://t.co/Pl5EKUsM55)
The III-V compound semiconductor supply chain is catching a broad bid as government and hyperscaler capital validates photonic materials as critical infrastructure. IQE ripped +32.70% on intensifying takeover speculation, while the parabolic large-caps (LITE, CIEN) digest their monster runs with healthy profit-taking.

1. Optical Transceivers & Active Networking
CIEN, LITE: Cooling off after parabolic Q1 runs. LITE gained 90.7% in Q1 alone post-NVIDIA's $2B investment and S&P 500 inclusion. Today's pullback is position trimming, not thesis deterioration.
AAOI, FN, COHR, GLW, VIAV: FN leading at +4.19% on fiber demand. COHR flat after its own massive rally. VIAV soft.
2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Foundries
IQE: Day's standout. Lazard-advised strategic review entering final stages with multiple bidders circling the entire company and individual asset packages.
AXTI: +3.53%, structural InP supply bottleneck beneficiary as hyperscalers lock up EML capacity through 2027+.
TSEM, GFS, SOI, ATEYY, FPLSF: TSEM steady at +2.89% on SiPho foundry momentum. Rest positive but muted.
3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials & PICs
ALMU: Today's catalyst leader. $4M government contract validates III-V-on-silicon integration. Non-dilutive funding is key given the active ATM shelf.
SIVEF, SIVE: Sivers ripping across both listings on InP laser positioning and Q4 2026 LiDAR production ramp catalyst.
LWLG, POET, BESIY: LWLG +7.45% catching speculative flow. POET +3.69%. BESIY fading.
4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test & Infrastructure
FORM, AEHR: Both tracking SiPho capacity buildout. FORM +3.57%, AEHR +1.75%.
KEYS, TER, MRVL: Grinding higher on test and DSP/PAM4 demand. TER lagging at +0.58%.

The AI Photonics & Optical Infrastructure sector is showing explosive bullish momentum, led by a 25.59% surge in $LWLG. Lightwave Logic is running on a multi-catalyst stack built since March. The core trigger is the development agreement with Tower Semiconductor, integrating LWLG's Perkinamine EO polymer modulators into Tower's PH18 silicon photonics PDK targeting 110GHz+ bandwidth for 400G-per-lane applications, with multiple engineering tapeouts scheduled through 2026. This is not a vague MOU. Tower's PH18 is a production-grade platform, and Tower itself recently disclosed a separate NVIDIA collaboration on 1.6T optical modules. LWLG also integrated its modulator into GlobalFoundries' GDSFactory design kit for 200G/400G SiPho, and completed an initial tapeout with SilTerra and Luceda Photonics with results expected mid-2026. The stock is up 764% over the past year as the market reprices LWLG from R&D-stage materials play to foundry-embedded modulator platform with a credible 2027 volume production path (https://t.co/MOtKo5Er07).

The broader value chain is bid as AI infra spending accelerates toward $690B in 2026 and the 800G-to-1.6T transceiver migration outstrips supply through mid-2027. $AAOI continues its April tear on $124M in cumulative hyperscaler orders, while $IQE and $SOI are catching parabolic bids as investors chase upstream InP exposure.

IQE, SOI, AXTI, TSEM, GFS: IQE +23.33% and SOI +19.01% as investors aggressively front-run the InP supply deficit. TSEM -0.11%, noise given Tower's direct role in the LWLG catalyst.

text: Photonics boom is real..
embedded_text:
$CRDO is acquiring DustPhotonics in a $750M silicon photonics deal that also includes stock. 

The deal expands Credo deeper into optical connectivity across 400G to 3.2T with the combined optical business expected to exceed $500M in FY2027. https://t.co/fYprDnh1zS

🔹 Credo: Acquiring DustPhotonics for $750M + stock
🔹 Speeds: 400G, 800G, 1.6T, future 3.2T
🔹 Combined optical revenue: Expected $500M+ by FY2027
🔹 DustPhotonics: Fabless, ~70 employees — pure IP acquisition

💥 Why this deal is significant:
🔹 MRVL Barclays upgrade: Optical ports doubling 2026+2027
🔹 ANET Rosenblatt upgrade: XPO optical strategy + Google/Anthropic wins
🔹 LITE: Sold out through 2028 within 2 quarters
🔹 NOW: Credo paying $750M for 70-person silicon photonics startup = optical is THAT strategic
🔹 3.2T = future standard = DustPhotonics is building the optical stack for the next generation
🔹 $500M combined optical revenue FY2027 = massive step-up for Credo

🔸 The optical supercycle M&A phase:
🔹 Credo x DustPhotonics = $750M
🔹 MRVL Barclays OW $150 (optical ports doubling)
🔹 LITE Aletheia $1,225 (sold out 2028)
🔹 SNDK Evercore $1,200 (storage optical layer)
🔹 AMAT 2nm tools for optical chip layers
🔹 Every AI chip, every data center = needs optical. M&A is how you get it fast.

📊 Watch:
🟢 $CRDO — $750M acquisition + $500M revenue FY2027 = major optical connectivity player
🟢 $LITE $MRVL $ANET — optical supercycle M&A validates the sector
🟡 Integration risk — 70 employees, all IP = talent retention = key risk

⚡ $750 million for 70 people and their photonics IP.

That’s how valuable optical connectivity has become in the AI era.

text:
Another company tying itself to multiple photonics-driven themes is $CRDO. Similar move to $MRVL.

Credo is up 9% AH following its acquisition of DustPhotonics, positioning itself into high-speed optical connectivity for AI data center interconnects. As clusters scale and bandwidth demand shifts from copper toward optics beyond the rack level, assets like DSPs and optical modules become increasingly strategic.

For comparison, Marvell is up nearly 50% since announcing its acquisition of Celestial AI, which added a silicon-photonics platform aimed at scaling memory bandwidth and chip-to-chip connectivity inside next-generation AI systems.
embedded_text: Credo $CRDO is acquiring silicon photonics company DustPhotonics for $750M in cash plus about 0.92M shares, with additional earnout shares tied to milestones. The deal should help lift its combined optical revenue to more than $500M in fiscal 2027. https://t.co/oHLu3177XL

text:
The AI infrastructure bottleneck nobody talks about.

As AI clusters scale past copper's physical limits, optical photonics becomes the critical layer.

These companies are building the infrastructure layer that powers the next generation of AI systems—and the investment signals that come with them.

A massive data throughput upgrade is coming. The question is which companies capture the value.

text:
I've been quietly watching on the sideline for a couple of months now.

Everybody and their grandmas are hyping photonics (and for a very good reason, no doubt on that!) but there are truly a few people doing the actual work and a ton of parrots.

A handful of persons truly do the research and others just copy/paste it, reformulate it, ask AI to generate something they didn't even read and then post.

text:
Spot on by @Chaamacoo. 🎯

People keep trying to put $SIVE / $SIVEF in a single box. It’s not just an "AI stock", a "defense stock", or a "telecom stock." It is the foundational silicon and photonics chokepoint for all of them.

Think of them as the "Intel Inside" of the next hardware supercycle. If you want to understand the true TAM, here are the actual end-products that will rely on "Sivers Inside" to function:

🧠 AI Datacenters: Next-gen 1.6T LRO pluggable transceivers (Jabil/Marvell) & Co-Packaged Optics.

 🪖 NATO Defense & SATCOM: @allspaceltd  multi-orbit terminals for Special Forces (SOF) & Navy frigates. 

🚁 Drones (UAVs): Stealth flat-panel SATCOM connectivity and FMCW LiDAR navigation. 

🗼 Next-Gen Telecom: 5G/6G massive MIMO antennas & Fixed Wireless Access (FWA). 

🚄 Infrastructure: High-speed "Track-to-Train" gigabit broadband networks. 

🚗 Autonomous Vehicles: Advanced LiDAR sensor arrays for self-driving cars. 

⌚ Wearables & Health: Biometric optical sensors (think next-gen Apple Watch & health trackers).

You aren't buying a single theme hoping it plays out. You are buying the underlying crossroads of tomorrow's technology. The sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation could be huge. ⏳🔥
$SIVE $SIVEF #Semiconductors #Photonics

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "6646", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045961814413115759", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4378", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Photonics is a stack! 
$AXTI $AIXA $TSEM $AEHR $LITE $GLW

Here is a clean map of the sector.
Please bookmark & share! 

A laser, a photonic chip, a finished module, and a deployed AI network all sit at different points in one long chain. 

Different companies get paid for totally different reasons and on totally different timelines.

I just updated my layer framework so you can instantly see where every major name fits that I track (and why the AI buildout lifts the whole stack).

Quick map:

Layer 1 (Materials & Platforms): $AXTI $ALMU $LWLG $IQE
Layer 2 (Epitaxy Tools): $AIXA $ALRIB $VECO
Layer 3 (Foundries & Scale): $TSEM $FN $SANM
Layer 4 (Test & Yield): $AEHR $FORM $COHU $TER $VIAV
Layer 5 (Devices & Engines): $LITE $COHR $AAOI $POET $SIVE $MTSI
Layer 6 (Connectivity & Networks): $CRDO $MRVL $SMTC $CIEN $NOK $GLW

There are many more names in the industry that didn't make this list. 

Which are you tracking the closest?

Agreed—the signals are aligning faster than consensus expects. Photonics holding RS while semis pause and energy narratives mature points to the transfer layer waking up. 

Group rotation in optical components + capex pivots toward interconnect efficiency (not just more FLOPs) is the tell. COHR’s positioning in that bandwidth chokepoint looks timely.

What’s your read on the next confirmation trigger—earnings language or order flow?

@grok It’s already happening @grok Semis are crowded.
Energy is understood. Now you’re seeing:

-photonics holding strength
-group rotation instead of single names
-capex shifting toward interconnect + bandwidth

That’s the early phase. Not obvious yet…but it’s forming.

text:
The 3-year rule holds for commoditizable bottlenecks: DRAM, NAND, legacy logic. It breaks for capital-intensive or IP-protected ones: ASML EUV is 9 years and counting, TSMC leading-edge over a decade, Soitec/Shin-Etsu on SOI wafers 15+ years. Photonics supply chain fits the second bucket, not the first.

On 'meme-like' valuations, the right benchmark isn't Street targets, it's what the market already pays for rerated peers. Coherent trades at ~8.4x EV/Sales, growing 22% Y/Y, with Nvidia owning $2B of equity. That's the anchor for a fully-discovered photonics name this cycle.

Checking the supply chain against it: Soitec ~4x EV/Sales (50% discount to COHR), IQE ~6x (in-play takeover), LPKF ~2x. Those are meaningful discounts to the rerated comp, not premiums.

Where you're right: names that have run 500%+ in months on sub-$50M revenue can't be defended on peer-relative grounds either. Those are real outliers worth separating from the broader supply-chain story.

Duration holds, most of the supply chain trades at discounts to the rerated peer, some individual names are genuinely overextended. Bottleneck durability and single-name multiples aren't the same question.

text:
Most finance Twitter talks about semiconductors.

Almost nobody on finance Twitter has actually built one.

Trade Whisperer has 21 years in HBM, DRAM, and NAND plus graduate research in silicon photonics and lasers.

His AMA is open right now.

If you have any real exposure to $MU, $SNDK, $TSM, or the broader semi complex, this is a rare window.

Here's why it matters.

The memory thesis driving Micron's projected out-earning of Amazon and Meta by 2027 sits on one question.

Can Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron ramp HBM supply fast enough to capture current AI demand without triggering the classic memory glut cycle?

Analysts have views. Sell-side has models. Bulls and bears argue on podcasts.

Almost none of them have ever been inside a fab.

Trade Whisperer answers questions like "how long does HBM qualification actually take" and "why can't China catch up" from the engineering floor rather than the spreadsheet.

From the thread already running:

China catching up to HBM is "extremely hard. It's a moving target. If they ever figure out HBM3, others will be producing HBM4E by then."

Fab expansions "take decades for 100% utilization." The supply glut fear is priced for a cycle that can't physically happen as fast as the market assumes.

He's bullish on SK Hynix over Samsung specifically because of HBM3E leadership. That's not a trading view. That's an engineer's view on who actually makes the best chip right now.

These are the kind of reads that generate edge in semi positions.

Not because he's predicting prices. Because he's explaining physical constraints that determine what the prices eventually have to reflect.

If you hold MU or SNDK or any semi name, spend 20 minutes reading his replies.

Ask him something specific. Depth of qualification cycles. HBM4 timeline. Silicon photonics for interconnect. How packaging capacity actually constrains supply.

Finance Twitter is full of people who read earnings transcripts.

A semi engineer with 21 years of HBM experience doing an AMA is a different tier of information.

Use it while it's open.

Follow for more signals on when someone who actually knows something is worth listening to in public.

text:
Totally agree, photonics looks set to shine bright in the 2030s! 🚀
I love how it can be made using the same manufacturing lines as regular computer chips. That makes scaling up much easier. And combining it with terahertz tech on silicon for computing, communication, and even imaging all in one? Sounds like a powerful way forward.  Any interesting recent papers on this terahertz + photonics combo you’d recommend? Always fun chatting with someone who sees the potential too! ✨

text: @JackXJong Fair question. GlobalFoundries handles their wireless products, while WIN Semiconductors is used for high-volume photonics production, such as DFB lasers and arrays. The Glasgow fab is mainly focused on low-volume manufacturing, pilot runs, and continued technology development.

It is the company’s 100mm InP fab in Glasgow 🏭 Sivers owns and operates one of the few independent III-V photonic foundries with both advanced development capability and meaningful production capacity. This is not just an R&D line, but a qualified InP100 manufacturing platform with end-to-end capabilities — from epitaxy design and wafer processing to automated testing and qualification. The platform includes:  • Up to 5,000 wafer starts per year  • Automated test capacity of more than 2 million lasers per month  • ~$17M capex to expand toward ~$150M annual revenue capacity What makes this especially interesting is the etched-facet process with on-wafer optical coatings and testing. Rather than relying on slower, less efficient post-cleave workflows, Sivers can process, coat, and test on the full wafer before dicing — improving yield, cost, cycle time, and precision. Strategically, that matters. The in-house fab gives Sivers control over development, prototyping, qualification, and early-to-mid volume manufacturing, while partners like WIN Semiconductors provide a path to ultra-high-volume scale when needed. That combination supports the company’s shift from 1-to-1 to one-to-many: design once, qualify once, and sell repeatedly with known specs, shorter lead times, and better operating leverage. In an industry where many photonics players are fabless, owning this specialized manufacturing capability is a quiet but powerful advantage. Underrated foundation for $SIVE. text:
$AAOI is good for photonics and $CRDO for connectivity. Also $CLS and $COHR which does it all, and $AEHR for testing every semiconductor.

Software it's clearly $PLTR and $APP upside is massive as well as $NOW.

Defense - Drones are probably a better bet. I like $SWMR after the IPO.. price is more attractive with Erik Prince leading it. His connections and experience is bar none the best. Swarmer software runs on a drone as well increasing the TAM 100 fold.

Health - I was against this initially but the rise of peptides is real, so $HIMS and $IBRX for cancer in biotech.

Space - $PL (Google backed) and $RKLB is great. $ASTS is gonna have too much competition for me.

Robotics.. $TSLA - People need to start making that connection and just go long.

Insurance - I couldn't care less about. All scams. Probably United Healthcare though.

Cybersecurity is gonna be $CRWD - they're onboard for Anthropics company leadership board for security concerns around AI guardrails.

Quantum... still early for me to go all in, but $IONQ $INFQ are solid bets.

text:
No, not really similar in core tech. 

VIGO Photonics makes high-sensitivity HgCdTe (MCT) uncooled IR detectors/sensors for MWIR/LWIR detection—used in missiles, gas sensing, defense, and space (e.g. Curiosity rover). 

$LPTH focuses on IR optics (chalcogenide BlackDiamond lenses) + complete thermal imaging cameras/systems. Complementary pieces of the IR chain, not the same detection tech. 

(From the infographic + company sites)

@Frenchie_ Photonics is the angle, but the real story is aerospace.
MEMSCAP's pressure sensors go into planes and rockets - defense spending is booming and that's the actual catalyst here.

text: Photonics are taking off because most are small caps. They are easily modulated by the sudden investments that come in. Now having said that, the need for optics is REAL (reserach Ultra Rubin). Whole suupply chain is trying to figure how important they will become in the next 5 years. You need lasers but you need special materials for lasers but you also need special Co-Packaged-Optics for these to work in tandem with Silicon Chips... now you need more Pure Fiber Optics glass... now you need Optics Foundry as well. So yeah, the whole chain is moving. It's hype/real depending which company we are taking about. If psychology is what you're seeking stay invested on easy to understand optics players. I am staying with $LITE $COHR $GLW. $TSM $ASX for CPO.

Great team, great company.   Dust designs photonic ICs (PICs) and engines that are foundational to Silicon Photonics. Silicon Photonic pluggables integrate multiple optical functions – traditionally implemented with discrete components – into a single component called a PIC. This saves space and power while improving scalability.     Perhaps most importantly, PICs can feed multiple optical lanes with a smaller number of lasers, relative to traditional approaches typically requiring a discrete laser for each lane (e.g., 8x200G lasers for a 1.6T module). Timely given we are heading into a severe shortage of lasers. Please note that there will still be a laser shortage even if Dust’s PICs are broadly adopted.   Thermal and power-density limitations for pluggables will also eventually make adding more and more lasers impractical.  Silicon Photonics and PICs essentially create another axis for scaling bandwidth. And obviously, long-term, Silicon Photonics is essential for CPO.   PICs feel similar to RF in the early days of cellular - quite a bit of black magic. There are only a few companies successfully supplying datacom PICs today at scale – Dust is one of them.     Ronnen and team have been excellent operators and partners, and their products are well respected across the datacom ecosystem. Over $500m in optical revenue for Credo is material and I do think HyperLume was a smart bet for them - starting to hear more positive feedback about MicroLEDs from our venture portcos and the hyperscalers. Copper, CPO, Pluggables, MicroLEDs should all win for the foreseeable future in different applications in different hyperscaler networking topologies: networking is taking huge share of the datacenter BoM everywhere.   Coherent training clusters are increasing in size to enable ever larger models. A larger coherent cluster is much more networking intensive. And then the larger models trained on these larger clusters require larger switched scale-up domains to inference economically, which is again more networking intensive. Rubin and Trainium 4 will have much larger switched scale-up domains and we may need these systems deployed at scale to enable the broad availability of 10 trillion plus parameter models like Mythos. Networking, especially switched scale-up networking, should be the fastest growing part of the datacenter for the next several years.   Switched scale-up networking (almost all copper with some optical beginning late next year) > scale across (optical obviously) > scale-out (first place for CPO) from a growth perspective next 3 years imo. We will be using copper well into the 2030s and somewhat ironic that the growth of optical is likely to drive accelerated growth for copper in the near term relative to the strange zero-sum thinking I occasionally see here and in some sell-side notes."}}}, "rest_id": "2044410644301046031", "source": "Twitter for iPad", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "120977", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2044492573645869108", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "319", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
A lot of people seem to think photonics has already had its big run.
They’re missing the next leg.

Stocks like $AAOI and $LITE exploded on the current 800G pluggable wave — a lot of that growth is now priced in.

The real next chapter is CPO - (Co-Packaged Optics).

CPO solves the massive power and heat problems in AI data centers way better than today’s pluggables. Hyperscalers are already designing their future clusters around it. The big ramp will be 2027–2028.

The companies that actually enable CPO — $SIVE (critical lasers), $SOI (substrates), $TSEM (foundry), $AEHR (testing) — are still trading at valuations that don’t reflect how big this shift can become.

CPO is the layer where the next major move in photonics will come from IMO.

text:
Been digging through European photonics the last few weeks.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room.

Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly.

LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap.

The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line.

Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins.

There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet.

One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment.

One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open.

Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream.

H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early.

More to come.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room. Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly. LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap. The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line. Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins. There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet. One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment. One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open. Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream. H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early. More to come."}}}, "rest_id": "2044883190188568579", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "88643", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
And another one!!

$TER Teradyne has acquired TestInsight, a leader in semiconductor test development, validation, and conversion software.

So they first acquired my former employer Quantifi Photonics 🇳🇿, then my other former employer MutliLane 🇱🇧 in a JV, and now TestInsight 🇮🇱.

Teradyne picked up TestInsight, a software outfit that has been around since 1999 and sits right between chip design and ATE production test (pattern conversion, validation and virtual testing). 

They have been the default software partner for both Teradyne and Advantest (sayonara 👋🏻) since 2001, which covers roughly 90% of the SoC test market. 

For the MultiLane JV and Quantifi, the read is complementary, not overlapping: Quantifi brought optical test, MultiLane handles high-speed electrical and SerDes, and TestInsight now adds the upstream software layer. Lined up like that, it is a pretty clean roll-up aimed at owning the full test stack for AI accelerators, switch ASICs and CPO. 

Advantest $ATEYY is the one that loses here. They just watched a neutral partner get absorbed by their main competitor.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Full press release from Teradyne below:

text:
Credo $CRDO is acquiring DustPhotonics for $750 million cash plus equity. This is the biggest move they've made.

My concern with CRDO was always copper. Their business was built on Active Electrical Cables, and the shift toward silicon photonics was the one thing that could break the thesis. This deal answers that.

DustPhotonics is a fabless Israeli company with 70 engineers and a SiPho PIC portfolio spanning 400G, 800G, and 1.6T with a 3.2T roadmap. Their L3C laser coupling technology lowers power, simplifies manufacturing, and removes the EML supply chain problem that has been holding the industry back.

CEO William Brennan said it plainly: "a vertically integrated connectivity platform that spans from copper to optical and from chip to cluster."

That is the whole thesis now. 
$CRDO $COHR $LITE $AAOI

text:
$CRDO — The Connectivity Stack Just Went Full Spectrum

Credo closed +12.35% today and ripped another 9% after hours to $146.80. The catalyst: a definitive agreement to acquire DustPhotonics for $750M cash + stock, bringing industry-leading Silicon Photonics PIC technology in-house.

This is a thesis-level event. 

Here’s why:

The Bear Case Just Died

The biggest overhang on CRDO was the copper-vs-optical debate — would optical interconnect eventually cannibalize their dominant AEC franchise? That question is now irrelevant. Credo owns both sides of the connectivity stack: AECs within racks, optical between racks and across pods. They don’t care which one wins. They win either way.

What DustPhotonics Brings

DustPhotonics has a differentiated SiPho PIC portfolio spanning 400G, 800G, 1.6T with a roadmap to 3.2T — already deployed at leading hyperscale AI clusters and in design for both NPO and CPO applications. This isn’t a speculative R&D bet. These are production-grade photonic integrated circuits sitting in live AI infrastructure today.

The Numbers

Credo now expects combined optical revenue (ZeroFlap transceivers + Optical DSPs + Silicon Photonics) to exceed $500M in fiscal 2027. For context, CRDO did ~$268M total revenue last quarter. Optical alone is on track to become a revenue pillar on par with the core AEC/IC franchise. The deal is expected accretive to non-GAAP EPS in FY27.

The Vertically Integrated Moat

Post-acquisition, Credo’s stack reads: SerDes → DSP → Silicon Photonics → System Integration. Electrical and optical. Scale-up and scale-out. No other pure-play connectivity company offers this full spectrum. Broadcom has optical but is a conglomerate. Marvell has optical but lacks the AEC dominance. CRDO is now the only vertically integrated connectivity pure-play purpose-built for AI datacenter fabric.

The Technical Setup

Price reclaimed the 50 SMA and blew through the 100 and 200 SMAs in a single session on 2x average volume. After hours puts it at $147, back inside the prior $140–165 consolidation zone from late 2025. This is conviction buying, not a dead cat bounce. Next resistance: $160–165.

Conference call tomorrow, April 14 at 1 PM ET. That’s your next catalyst — integration roadmap, design win pipeline, and FY27 guidance details.

When the market gives you a company that was already the AEC market leader with 75%+ share, and they go acquire the missing piece to own the entire AI connectivity stack from copper to light — you pay attention.

text:
$CRDO up ~9% AH. 

Stock had been getting crushed on fears that silicon photonics would make their copper-based solutions obsolete. 

Today they said: fine, we'll own photonics too, acquiring DustPhotonics for $750M cash. If you can't beat them, buy them. https://t.co/bg8tw4NEsW

Got it, Daniel — DREJ / @DanielREJackso1
 (Raytheon Photonics Engineer).  Since you asked to expand on photonics in Starlink lasers, here’s the deep, technical dive tailored to your expertise level. This builds directly on the Starlink specs we covered earlier (1550 nm ISLs, ~10,200 active satellites, 3 lasers per V2/V2 Mini, up to 200 Gbps per link). All data is current as of April 2026 from SpaceX disclosures, FCC filings, SPIE papers, and technical analyses.  I’m keeping this at a professional photonics-engineer level — no fluff, just the optics, lasers, modulation, PAT, and space-hardening realities you deal with every day at Raytheon.1. Wavelength Choice: Why 1550 nm C-Band (ITU DWDM Grid)Starlink uses the telecom C-band (~1530–1565 nm) for all operational ISLs — specifically ITU DWDM channels with 100 GHz spacing. Public analyses (from reverse-engineering of early terminals and coherent transceiver behavior) point to:  “Red” channel: ~1555.75 nm (ITU Ch. 27, 192.7 THz)  
“Blue” channel: ~1549.32 nm (ITU Ch. 35, 193.5 THz)

Photonics advantages in LEO:  Eye-safe at high power (Class 1M).  
Mature, low-loss fiber components (EDFAs, modulators, coherent receivers) directly transferable from terrestrial telecom.  
Excellent atmospheric compatibility if they ever push space-to-ground optical (though ISLs are vacuum-only).  
Low solar background noise in this band compared to 1064 nm (legacy ESA/Tesat systems).

This is a deliberate shift from older 1064 nm Nd:YAG systems — Starlink went all-in on COTS photonics for cost/scalability.2. Transmitter Chain: Coherent Transceivers + High-Power EDFAEach laser terminal is built around commercial coherent optical transceivers (widely believed to be space-hardened versions of Acacia/Nokia/Cisco CFP2-DCO or similar 200G modules).  Laser source: Narrow-linewidth DFB or external-cavity laser (~100 kHz linewidth typical for coherent).  
Modulation: Dual-polarization quadrature phase-shift keying (DP-QPSK) baseline; higher-rate links (up to 200 Gbps) use 16-QAM or higher-order formats. Baud rates push ~50–64 Gbaud in the latest V2 hardware.  
Amplification: External C-band EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier) with integrated high-power amp (HPA) + low-noise amp (LNA). These are miniaturized for space (some prototypes now <1U CubeSat size). Output power is boosted to achieve the required EIRP for 200 Gbps at inter-satellite ranges (hundreds to ~5,000 km).

The whole Tx chain is fiber-coupled to the free-space optics — classic photonics integration you’d recognize from directed-energy or comms payloads.3. Receiver Side: Coherent DetectionIntradyne coherent receiver with local oscillator (LO) laser phase-locked to the incoming signal.  
Balanced photodetectors + DSP for polarization demux, carrier recovery, and forward-error correction.  
This gives massive sensitivity advantage over direct-detection (DD-OOK) systems — critical for the narrow beams and long slant ranges in LEO.

4. Optical Terminal DesignAperture: Single common Tx/Rx aperture per terminal, ~10 cm diameter (eyeball estimates from public photos/CAD: 100 mm ±40 mm). Diffraction-limited beam divergence:
θ≈λD≈1.55 μm0.1 m≈15.5 μrad\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{D} \approx \frac{1.55\,\mu\mathrm{m}}{0.1\,\mathrm{m}} \approx 15.5\,\mu\mathrm{rad}\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{D} \approx \frac{1.55\,\mu\mathrm{m}}{0.1\,\mathrm{m}} \approx 15.5\,\mu\mathrm{rad}

(full-width half-max ~0.9–1 mrad in practice with truncation).  
3 lasers per satellite (V2/V2 Mini) — each with its own optical head for in-plane + cross-plane links.  
Beam steering: Coarse pointing via satellite attitude control + fine steering mirrors (piezo or MEMS fast-steering mirrors — FSMs). No big gimbals on the main aperture (mass/volume optimized).

5. PAT (Pointing, Acquisition & Tracking) — The Hardest Photonics Problem They Solved at ScaleThis is where Starlink’s photonics really shines (and where >250,000 acquisitions/day happen across the fleet):  Acquisition: Beacon lasers + quadrant photodiodes or focal-plane arrays for initial coarse lock. Orbit ephemeris (shared via RF or prior laser links) gives initial pointing vector.  
Fine pointing: MEMS or piezo FSMs for microradian accuracy. Closed-loop feedback from position-sensitive detectors.  
Tracking bandwidth: High enough to handle relative velocities (~7.5 km/s) and orbital dynamics. Link handovers every ~15–100 seconds depending on geometry.  
Uptime: >99% per link reported in fleet-wide data (42+ PB/day total laser traffic across thousands of terminals).

They achieved this with predictive PAT + massive redundancy in the mesh network — if one link drops, data reroutes instantly via other lasers.6. Space Environment & Hardening (Raytheon-Relevant)Radiation: COTS components get conformal coating, lead-free solder, and selective shielding.  
Thermal: Passive + active management — LEO thermal cycling is brutal on optics.  
Vibration/shock: Launch survival + on-orbit micro-vibration isolation for the FSMs.  
Power: Terminals draw ~30–100 W total (mostly EDFA + electronics).

7. The “Mini Laser” Variant (New 2025–2026)SpaceX now offers a smaller terminal for third-party sats (e.g., Muon Space Halo, Vast Haven-1):  25 Gbps at up to 4,000 km.  
Same 1550 nm coherent photonics core but lower power/aperture.  
Already flight-tested and being integrated into non-Starlink spacecraft.

Bottom Line for a Photonics EngineerStarlink didn’t invent new laser physics — they weaponized mature telecom photonics at constellation scale: C-band coherent transceivers + EDFAs + MEMS FSMs + ruthless systems engineering. The result is >10,000 laser terminals in orbit forming a true optical mesh backbone that carries petabytes daily with millisecond latency. It’s the largest deployed free-space optical network in history.This is exactly the kind of high-volume, cost-optimized photonics you see evolving in defense (directed energy, comms payloads) — except SpaceX did it commercially and at 100× scale.Want to go deeper?
